findings.description: The PDF uses a compact multi-column table layout with regional groupings separated by whitespace, while the HTML renders as a full-width single-row-per-state table without visual grouping separators between regions.
findings.evidence: PDF groups states by region (New England, Mid-Atlantic, Southeast, etc.) with blank row separators. HTML renders all rows uniformly without these regional spacing breaks.

findings.description: Table structure is preserved with correct row/column counts and data, but the regional grouping whitespace rows present in the PDF are absent in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has visible blank rows between state groupings (e.g., after Virgin Islands, after West Virginia, after Tennessee, etc.). HTML table has no such separators.

findings.description: USDA logo is present in both versions but styled differently - PDF shows the full USDA seal with green banner, HTML shows a smaller simplified version.
findings.evidence: PDF: Full-color USDA seal with 'United States Department of Agriculture' banner. HTML: Smaller grayscale/simplified USDA logo with text.

findings.description: Column headers have slightly different wording/formatting between PDF and HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF header: 'Official Fiscal Year 2019 CAPER' | HTML header: 'Official Fiscal Year 2019 CAPER'. PDF: 'Fiscal Year 2019 Cumulative October through June' | HTML: 'Fiscal Year 2019 Cumulative October through June'. Formatting is similar but PDF uses centered bold headers while HTML uses left-aligned headers.

findings.description: Footnotes at bottom have slightly different formatting but same content.
findings.evidence: PDF: footnotes appear at bottom of the table area with specific formatting. HTML: footnotes appear at the very bottom in smaller text, right-aligned.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in HTML - structural duplication
findings.evidence: The HTML contains two nested <main> tags, which is invalid HTML structure but does not affect content fidelity.
